title: - A GENERALIZED PROBABILITY KERNEL ON DIS - CRETE DISTRIBUTIONS AND ITS APPLICATION IN TWO SAMPLE TEST [PDF]

paragraphs.text:
Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

## - A GENERALIZED PROBABILITY KERNEL ON DIS - CRETE DISTRIBUTIONS AND ITS APPLICATION IN TWO SAMPLE TEST

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

We propose a generalized probability kernel(GPK) on discrete distributions with finite support. This probability kernel, defined as kernel between distributions instead of samples, generalizes the existing discrepancy statistics such as maximum mean discrepancy(MMD) as well as probability product kernels, and extends to more general cases. For both existing and newly proposed statistics, we estimate them through empirical frequency and illustrate the strategy to analyze the resulting bias and convergence bounds. We further propose power-MMD, a natural extension of MMD in the framework of GPK, illustrating its usage for the task of two-sample test. Our work connects the fields of discrete distribution-property estimation and kernel-based hypothesis test, which might shed light on more new possibilities.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

We focus on the two-sample problem, which is given two i.i.d samples _{x_ 1 _, x_ 2 _, ...xn} , {y_ 1 _, y_ 2 _, ..., yn}_ , could we infer the discrepancy between underlying distributions they are drawn from. For such a problem, the option of hypothesis test(two-sample test) is most popular, and a variety of statistics in estimating the discrepancy is proposed. In recent years, RKHS based method such as maximum mean discrepancy(MMD) has gained a lot of attention. (Gretton et al., 2012) has shown that in a universal-RKHS _F_ , MMD( _F,_ _**p** ,_ _**q**_ ) = 0 if and only if _**p**_ = _**q**_ , thus could be used for the two-sample hypothesis test. (Gretton et al., 2012) further provides unbiased estimator of MMD with fast asymptotic convergence rate, illustrating its advantages.

On the other hand, estimating distribution properties with plugin(empirical) estimators on discrete setting is an active research area in recent years, where people focus on problem settings with large support size but not so large sample size. The Bernstein polynomial technique is introduced to analyze the bias of the plugin estimators in (Yi & Alon, 2020), which provides remarkable progress on bias-reduction methods of the plugin estimators. It is thus interesting to ask if the plugin estimators could motivate new results for the RKHS-based two-sample test.

Another interesting topic is about the probability kernel, defined as kernel function over probabilities, instead of over samples. As is easily seen, any discrepancy measure of distribution _**p**_ and _**q**_ could potentially be valid probability kernels, not so much work focuses on this. While (Jebara et al., 2004) introduced the so called probability product kernels which generalize a variety of discrepancy measures, its properties remain further study.

Motivated by above observations, our work focuses on a specialized probability kernel function which is a direct generalization of sample-based RKHS methods such as MMD. We focus on using plugin-estimator as the default estimator of the kernel function we defined, and illustrate that with the help of Bernstein polynomial techniques, we could analyze the bias and convergence bounds of these plugin-estimators. Our work thus connects the fields of discrete distribution-property estimation and kernel-based hypothesis test, which brings interesting possibilities.

1

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

## 2 NOTATION

We use bold symbol _**p** ,_ _**q** ∈_ R _[k]_ to represent a probability function over a discrete support with support size _k_ , and _pi, qi_ represents the _i_ th entry of _**p**_ and _**q**_ . We use _{v_ 1 _, v_ 2 _, ..., vk}, vi ∈_ R _[d]_ to represent the support of _**p** ,_ _**q**_ . [ _k_ ] := _{_ 1 _,_ 2 _,_ 3 _..., k}_ represents the set of indices of elements in _{v_ 1 _, v_ 2 _, ..., vk}_ . We use _φ ◦_ ( _**p** ,_ _**q**_ ) to denote an element-wise function from R _[k] ×_ R _[k]_ to R _[k]_ , where ( _φ ◦_ ( _**p** ,_ _**q**_ )) _i_ = _φ ◦_ ( _pi, qi_ ) and _φ ◦_ _**p**_ to denote an element-wise function from R _[k]_ to R _[k]_ , where ( _φ ◦_ _**p**_ ) _i_ = _φ ◦ pi_ . With a slight abuse of notation, we denote _**p**[ρ]_ , _**p** −_ _**q**_ as element-wise function defined above. We use kernel( _**p** ,_ _**q**_ ) to denote kernel function which maps from R _[k] ×_ R _[k]_ to real value R. And kernel( _x, y_ ) _, x, y ∈_ R _[d]_ represents a kernel function from R _[d] ×_ R _[d]_ to real value R. We use _K_ to denote the gram matrix generated from kernel( _x, y_ ) on finite support _{v_ 1 _, v_ 2 _, ..., vk}_ , where _Kij_ = kernel( _vi, vj_ ). We use _{x_ 1 _, x_ 2 _, ..., xn} ∼_ _**p**_ and _{y_ 1 _, y_ 2 _, ..., yn} ∼_ _**q**_ to denote the samples from distribution _**p**_ and _**q**_ , where _n_ is the sample size.

## 3 GENERALIZED PROBABILITY KERNEL

Probability kernel function, defined as kernel function between distributions instead of samples, is a natural extension of the idea of kernel function in sample space.

**Definition 1.** _Given distribution_ _**p** and_ _**q** belongs to a family of discrete distribution with the same finite support {v_ 1 _, v_ 2 _, ..., vk}, vi ∈_ R _[d] , where k is the support size, we define the probability kernel function as_ PK( _**p** ,_ _**q**_ ) _, which is a kernel function maps from_ R _[k] ×_ R _[k] to real value_ R _._

Many discrepancy measures, such as MMD, can serve as probability kernel functions, but people usually don’t use the term of probability kernel function when describing them. The reason is that for most of the time, we only consider a limited number of distributions, and do not need or have the resources to navigate through all the distributions within the family. For example, when looking into the two-sample problem, we usually assume two samples _{x_ 1 _, x_ 2 _, ..., xn} ∈_ R _[d]_ and _{y_ 1 _, y_ 2 _, ..., yn} ∈_ R _[d]_ are i.i.d drawn from two distributions _**p**_ and _**q**_ , and use the discrepancy measure MMD[ _F,_ _**p** ,_ _**q**_ ] to determine if _**p**_ and _**q**_ are indistinguishable in the RKHS _F_ . We do not consider all other distributions in _F_ that is irrelevant to our samples! So far the idea of kernel function between distributions is in practice not so much useful, however, here in this paper, we propose, when considering the plugin-estimator of many of the existing discrepancy measures, it is beneficial to view them as probability kernel functions.

## 3.1 DEFINATION OF GENERALIZED PROBABILITY KERNEL

**Definition 2** (Generalized probability kernel) **.** _Given the family S of discrete distribution on support {v_ 1 _, v_ 2 _, .., vk} where vi ∈_ R _[d] . Let F be a unit ball in a universal-RKHS H with associated continuous kernel_ RK( _x, y_ ) _, where for any x ∈_ R _[d] and y ∈_ R _[d] ,_ RK( _x, y_ ) _maps from_ R _[d] ×_ R _[d] to_ R _. We denote gram matrix Kij_ = RK( _vi, vj_ ) _._

_The generalized probability kernel function on distribution_ _**p** ,_ _**q** ∈S is_ GPK _F ,φ_ ( _**p** ,_ _**q**_ ) = _φ ◦_ ( _**p** ,_ _**q**_ ) _Kφ ◦_ ( _**q** ,_ _**p**_ ) _[T]_ =[�] _i∈_ [ _k_ ] � _j∈_ [ _k_ ] _[φ][ ◦]_[(] _[p][i][, q][i]_[)] _[K][ij][φ][ ◦]_[(] _[q][j][, p][j]_[)] _where φ ◦_ ( _**p** ,_ _**q**_ ) _is an element-wise mapping function on discrete distribution_ _**p** ,_ _**q** ∈S, which maps from_ R _[k] ×_ R _[k] to_ R _[k] ,_
Obviously, under this definition, the GPK is a symmetric probability kernel function where GPK _F ,φ_ ( _**p** ,_ _**q**_ ) = GPK _F ,φ_ ( _**q** ,_ _**p**_ )

Mapping function _φ_ represent a great amount of possibilities. For most cases, we need to narrow down the region and equipped it with some convenient properties so that the GPK measure could be useful.
One example is for the measurement of discrepancy, where we want GPK _F ,φ_ ( _**p** ,_ _**q**_ ) = 0 if and only if _**p**_ = _**q**_ .

**Definition 3** (discrepancy probability kernel) **.** _Let S be a family of discrete distribution_ _**p** ∈S on support {v_ 1 _, v_ 2 _, ..., vk}. A discrepancy probability kernel is a kernel function_ PK( _**p** ,_ _**q**_ ) _that_ PK( _**p** ,_ _**q**_ ) = 0 _if and only if_ _**p**_ = _**q**_

**Theorem 1.** GPK _F ,φ_ ( _**p** ,_ _**q**_ ) _with the mapping function φ that satisfies:_

_is a discrepancy probability kernel._

_Proof._

_K_ is a semipositive definite matrix, thus by definition of positive definite matrix, _**v** K_ _**v**[T] ≥_ 0, where equality holds if and only if _**v**_ = **0** , and since _**v**_ = _φ ◦_ ( _**p** ,_ _**q**_ ), this condition further means _φ ◦_ ( _**p** ,_ _**q**_ ) = 0, which holds if and only if _**p**_ = _**q**_ .

Another example is the polynomial GPK, which is our main focus of this paper. Such a subclass of GPK is interesting since we can build unbiased estimators of it using techniques of Bernstein polynomial in (Qian et al., 2011). As we will show in section 5., we also have analyzable convergence bounds for the resulting unbiased estimators, illustrating its potential usage for applications such as two-sample test.

**Definition 4** (polynomial GPK) **.** _The polynomial GPK is the subset of GPK that equipped with the o mapping function φ that is polynomial in_ _**p** and_ _**q** : φ ◦_ ( _**p** ,_ _**q**_ ) =[�] _[o] l_ =0 � _s_ =0 _[α][l,s]_ _**[p]**[l]_ _**[q]**[s][where][ o][ ∈]_[Z] _is the degree of the polynomial, and al,s ∈_ R _is the coefficient_

Below we give some examples of polynomial GPK, which include MMD proposed in (Gretton et al., 2012), and the newly proposed power-MMD in this paper, which is a natural extension of MMD, from the view point of probability kernels.

## 3.1.1 EXAMPLE 1: MMD AS MEMBER OF POLYNOMIAL GPK

Given discrete distribution _**p** ,_ _**q**_ with support _{v_ 1 _, v_ 2 _, ..., vk}_ , we can rewrite MMD with distribution probability function _pi, qi_ :
Where _φl ◦_ ( _**p** ,_ _**q**_ ) = _**p** −_ _**q**_ , _H_ is the RKHS defined in MMD literature, and _f_ is the function that maps _vi_ to _H_ .
GPK _F ,φl_ ( _**p** ,_ _**q**_ ) is a special case of polynomial GPK where _α_ 1 _,_ 0 = 1, _α_ 0 _,_ 1 = _−_ 1, and all other 0.

## 3.1.2 EXAMPLE 2: PRODUCT GPK AS MEMBERS OF POLYNOMIAL GPK

**Definition 5** (product GPK) **.** _Let_ _**p** and_ _**q** be probability distributions on support {v_ 1 _, v_ 2 _, ..., vk}, and l ∈_ Z _be nonnegative integer. The product GPK is a subset of polynomial GPK where αl,_ 0 = 1 _, and all other coefficients are_ 0 _. the corresponding mapping function is: φ_ ( _**p** ,_ _**q**_ ) = _**p**[l]_

The probability product kernel as in (Jebara et al., 2004) is a special case of product GPK where _K_ is a identity matrix.

## 3.1.3 EXAMPLE 3: POWER-MMD AS MEMBERS OF POLYNOMIAL GPK

Another interesting subset of polynomial GPK is the one extends MMD case into a power form and we denote it as power-MMD:

**Definition 6** (power-MMD) **.** _Let_ _**p** and_ _**q** be probability distributions on support {v_ 1 _, v_ 2 _, ..., vk} and ρ ∈_ Z _be a positive integer. then the power-MMD is a subset of polynomial GPK where αρ,_ 0 = 1 _, α_ 0 _,ρ_ = _−_ 1 _, and all other coefficients are_ 0 _. the corresponding mapping function is: φ_ ( _**p** ,_ _**q**_ ) = _**p**[ρ] −_ _**q**[ρ]_

Apparently, MMD is a special case of power-MMD where _ρ_ = 1, and power-MMD satisfies the requirement in Theorem 1, thus has the potential usage of discrepancy measure. In section 5., we will show that power-MMD has unbiased estimator with analyzable convergence bounds thus could be used for two-sample test.

## 3.2 DISCUSSION OF GPK IN DISCRETE SETTING

As one may easily notice, the definition of GPK includes a gram matrix generated by the kernel function RK( _vi, vj_ ) which measures the discrepancy between _vi, vj ∈{v_ 1 _, v_ 2 _, .., vk}_ . While considering the cases of categorical distribution, values of discrete variables does not relate to any notion of distance, this raises the question: how the introduced gram matrix will be beneficial in any cases?

The answer is twofold: 1. Many natural processes produce discrete distributions where there possibly exists a similarity measure in values which imply the similarity in frequencies of occurrence(probability values). For example, in the field of natural language process(NLP), one may treat words as atomic units with no notion of similarity between words, as these are represented as indices in a vocabulary. However, given large number of training samples, similarity measure between words could be made possible using techniques such as words2vec(Mikolov et al., 2013). Such techniques generally result in better performance and have become the important preprocessing techniques for NLP tasks(Goodfellow et al., 2016). 2. As there are cases where the values of discrete variables are totally irrelevant, or people may use kernel function RK( _vi, vj_ ) which doesn’t correctly imply the similarity in probability values, the GPK framework may still capture the similarity between distributions. One example is the case of MMD, which is, as we discussed above, an element of GPK family. As proved in (Gretton et al., 2012), MMD is a distribution free measurement between two samples, which means no matter what kind of _**p** ,_ _**q**_ and kernel( _x, y_ ), we have, the MMD[2] _F_[(] _**[p]**[,]_ _**[ q]**_[)][ measure will be][ 0][ if and only if] _**[ p]**_[=] _**[q]**_[.][However, the bad choice of kernel function] does have a negative effect on convergence bounds of the empirical estimator proposed in (Gretton et al., 2012), and will influence the results of two-sample test. For this reason, we mainly focus on dataset with known relativity measures in our experiment section.

## 4 PLUGIN-ESTIMATOR FOR GPK

So far we have defined the GPK and discussed some subsets of GPK with potential usage of two-sample test. Next we discuss how to build an estimator, given a member of GPK. In this section, we propose the plugin-estimator, which based on the count of occurrence of each value _vi ∈{v_ 1 _, v_ 2 _, ..., vk}_ in samples _{x_ 1 _, x_ 2 _, ..., xn} ∈_ _**p**_ or _**q**_ . We illustrate that by doing so, the techniques of Bernstein polynomial in (Qian et al., 2011) could be used to help building unbiased estimators for any members of polynomial GPK. Furthermore, we provide analyzable convergence bounds of these estimators.

We begin with the definition of plugin-estimators:

**Definition 7.** _Suppose we have i.i.d samples of distribution_ _**p** as Xn_ 1 := _{x_ 1 _, x_ 2 _, ..., xn_ 1 _} ∼_ _**p** and Xn_ 2 := _{xn_ 1+1 _, xn_ 1+2 _, ..., xn_ 1+ _n_ 2 _} ∼_ _**p** . And also the i.i.d samples of distribution_ _**q** as Ym_ 1 := _{y_ 1 _, y_ 2 _, ..., ym_ 1 _} ∼_ _**q** and Ym_ 2 := _{ym_ 1+1 _, ym_ 1+2 _, ..., ym_ 1+ _m_ 2 _} ∼_ _**q** ._

## 4.1 POLYNOMIAL GPK WITH UNBIASED PLUGIN-ESTIMATORS

One of our main contributions of this paper is the proposal that we can always find an unbiased plugin-estimator for any members in polynomial GPK family. The basic idea is that we can analyze the expectation of plugin-estimators through Bernstein polynomial, and use the existing results of (Qian et al., 2011) to build the unbiased plugin estimators.

_Proof._ The basic idea is directly using the result of Bernstein polynomial in (Qian et al., 2011) to build unbiased estimators. We put our formal proof in appendix
For notation simplicity, we define the plugin-estimator discussed above to be the default-pluginestimator for polynomial GPK:
**Definition 8** (default-plugin-estimator for polynomial GPK) **.** _The plugin-estimator defined in Theorem 2 is the default-plugin-estimator for polynomial GPK._

_This plugin-estimator, according to Theorem 2, is an unbiased estimator_

## 4.2 DEVIATION BOUND OF PLUGIN-ESTIMATORS
Another topic about plugin-estimator is its deviation bound. We directly use the McDiamid’s inequality to derive the default-plugin-estimator for polynomial GPK:

## 5 EXAMPLE: POWER-MMD AS A NATURAL EXTENSION TO MMD FROM GPK VIEWPOINT

In this section, we mainly discuss power-MMD as defined in 3.1.3. We analyze the bias and convergence bound of its plugin-estimators using the techniques we introduced so far, illustrating that such a natural extension to MMD from GPK viewpoint could be beneficial for two-sample test.

## 5.1 PLUGIN-ESTIMATORS OF POWER-MMD

As we already discussed in section 4.1.3, power-MMD is a subset of polynomial GPK. According to Theorem 2, any member GPK _F ,φρ_ ( _**p** ,_ _**q**_ ) in power-MMD has a default-plugin-estimator with the mapping function _fφ_ ( _Si,n_ 1 _, Si,m_ 1) = _gρ_ ( _Ni, n_ 1) _− gρ_ ( _Mi, m_ 1)

**Remark 3.1.** _When ρ_ = 1 _, the power-MMD return to the original MMD case. Remarkably, the default-plugin-estimator of this case is equivalent to the linear time statistic_ MMD[2] _l[proposed][in] (Gretton et al., 2012):_ GPK _E_ [ _F, φl, X, Y_ ] = MMD[2] _l_[[] _[F][, X, Y]_[ ]] _[For][details][of][the][derivation,][see] appendix_

## 5.3 TWO-SAMPLE TEST USING POWER-MMD

**Corollary 3.3.** _A hypothesis test of level α for the null hypothesis_ _**p**_ = _**q** has the acceptance region_ ��� GPK _E_ [ ~~_√_~~ _FZ,φρ,X,Y_ ] ��� _<_ � 12 _[log]_[(] � _α_ 2 � _−_ 1) _Where Z is defined in Corollary 3.1_

The two-sample test for power-MMD then follows this procedure: 1. calculate _v_ = ��� GPK _E_ [ ~~_√_~~ _FZ,φρ,X,Y_ ] ���. 2. check if _v <_ � 12 _[log]_[(] � _α_ 2 � _−_ 1), if so, accept the null hypothesis, otherwise reject the null hypothesis.
Next we analyze the performance of our proposed two-sample test under two cases: _ρ_ = 1 and _ρ >_ 1

## 5.3.1 _ρ_ = 1 CASE

For _ρ_ = 1 case, since GPK _E_ [ _F, φ_ 1 _, X, Y_ ] is equivalent to MMD[2] _l_[[] _[F][, φ]_[1] _[, X, Y]_[ ]][, the only difference] between our proposal and that of Gretton et al. (2012) is the convergence bound. According to Remark 3.2, we provide a tighter bound for the test statistic, thus we will certainly have a better performance using power-MMD.

## 5.3.2 _ρ >_ 1 CASE

We need to answer two questions for the _ρ >_ 1 case: 1. when applying power-MMD in practice, is the proposed statistics numerical stable? 2. will the performance of two-sample test gets better when _ρ_ gets larger?

For the question of numerical stability, since _gρ_ ( _Ni, n_ ) _≤_ � _Nni_ � _ρ_ , the term will exponentially decrease with the increase of _ρ_ . This effect will cause numerical problem when _Ni ≪ n_ and _ρ_ is large. One solution is to find an upper-bound of ��� GPK _E_ [ ~~_√_~~ _FZ,φρ,X,Y_ ] ��� which is numerical stable.

**Corollary 3.4.** _Consider the simplest case where n_ 1 = _n_ 2 = _m_ 1 = _m_ 2 = _n. Define_

To answer the question related to the performance of two-sample test when _ρ_ get larger, we need to analyze the case when _**p**_ = _**q**_ , if ��� GPK _E_ [ ~~_√_~~ _FZ,φρ,X,Y_ ] ��� increase with the increase of _ρ_ . Unfortunately, there is no clear answer to this.

## 6 SUMMARY

To summarize, we introduce the framework of generalized probability kernel(GPK). While GPK represents a large family of probability kernels, we focus on polynomial GPK since all members of such subset of GPK have unbiased plugin-estimators. Remarkably, a natural extension of MMD from the viewpoint of polynomial GPK, which we call power-MMD, could be used for two-sample test. Theoretical study shows that for _ρ_ = 1 case, power-MMD outperforms linear time MMD proposed in Gretton et al. (2012), and the performance of _ρ >_ 1 case is left for future work. For members of GPK which do not belong to polynomial GPK, it is not easy to design an unbiased estimators. However, bais reduction techniqes proposed in (Yi et al., 2018) and (Yi & Alon, 2020) could be used, and we still have the chance to apply two-sample test with the resulting estimators. Such a possibility is also left for future work.

zero_shot__gemini-3-flash-preview.label: Zero Shot (gemini-3-flash-preview)
zero_shot__gemini-3-flash-preview.overall_feedback: The paper introduces a 'Generalized Probability Kernel' (GPK) for discrete distributions, notably extending MMD to 'power-MMD' and providing a framework for unbiased estimation via Bernstein polynomials. While the theoretical connection between discrete property estimation and kernel methods is interesting, the paper suffers from several technical inconsistencies—specifically a breakdown in the proof of Theorem 1, a significant typo/error in the definition of the GPK estimator (Definition 7), and an unexamined growth in the variance/convergence bounds for the 'power-MMD' case which potentially offsets the claimed benefits of the 'tighter' bound.

comments.title: Logical gap in Theorem 1 proof
comments.quote: K is a semipositive definite matrix, thus by definition of positive definite matrix, vKv^{[T]} \ge 0, where equality holds if and only if v = 0 , and since v = \phi \circ (p, q), this condition further means \phi \circ (p, q) = 0, which holds if and only if p = q .
comments.explanation: The authors define $K$ as a semi-positive definite (SPD) matrix (the Gram matrix of a kernel). However, they conclude that $v^T K v = 0 \iff v = 0$. This is only true if $K$ is strictly positive definite. If $K$ has a zero eigenvalue, $v^T K v$ can be zero for non-zero $v$. For the GPK to be a valid 'discrepancy probability kernel' (meaning it is zero iff $p=q$), the kernel $RK$ must be characteristic on the support. While the authors mention 'universal-RKHS' earlier, the proof as written incorrectly equates semi-positive definiteness with the 'if and only if' condition of positive definiteness.

comments.title: Serious index error in GPK Estimator definition
comments.quote: GPK_E [F, \phi, X, Y] = \sum_{i \in [k]} \sum_{j \in [k]} f_{\phi}(S_{i, n_1}, S_{i, m_1}) K_{ij} f_{\phi}(S_{j, n_2}, S_{j, m_2})
comments.explanation: In Definition 7, the first mapping function is written as $f_{\phi}(S_{i, n_1}, S_{i, m_1})$. However, the second index of $f_{\phi}$ should likely relate to the $j$-th index of the Gram matrix given the structure of the GPK definition. More critically, the definition uses $S_{i, m_1}$ (a sample from $q$) inside the first term. If $\phi$ is a function like $p^\rho - q^\rho$ (power-MMD), the mapping function $f_{\phi}$ should be calculated on samples from the same distribution to be unbiased, or specifically partitioned. The mixing of $i$ and $j$ indices inside $f_{\phi}$ and the subscripts $n_1, m_1$ vs $n_2, m_2$ needs to align precisely with the expansion of $(p-q)K(p-q)^T$. As written, the formula is likely a typo that makes the estimator non-sensical (e.g., $f_{\phi}$ at index $i$ is usually a scalar, but here it seems to attempt to use both $p$ and $q$ samples in a way that doesn't match the expansion of a quadratic form).

comments.title: Ambiguous/Inconsistent Notation for element-wise powers
comments.explanation: The authors use square brackets with exponents to denote element-wise powers. However, in Definition 4 (Polynomial GPK), they use $p^l q^s$ inside a sum. In Definition 2, they define $\phi \circ (p, q)$ as a mapping to $\mathbb{R}^k$. The notation $p^{[\rho]}$ is later used in Example 3. It is slightly ambiguous whether $p^{[\rho]}$ refers to the $\ell_\rho$ norm or element-wise power. While context suggests element-wise power $p_i^\rho$, the lack of explicit definition combined with OCR artifacts makes the derivation in Section 5.2 difficult to verify for a reader not already familiar with Bernstein estimators.

comments.title: Unjustified claim regarding tighter bounds
comments.quote: Remark 3.2. recall in (Gretton et al., 2012), the deviation bound for linear time estimator ... Interestingly, this bound is the same as the case \rho = 1... Our bound generalize the bound in (Gretton et al., 2012) and provide a tighter version.
comments.explanation: The authors claim their bound is 'tighter' than Gretton's. However, their estimator uses four splits of the data ($n_1, n_2, m_1, m_2$), effectively reducing the 'n' used in the denominator of the convergence bound compared to the standard MMD linear estimator which uses pairs. Furthermore, for $\rho > 1$, while the bound formula might look similar, the constant $Z$ involving coefficients of Bernstein polynomials and $\tau$ constants grows significantly. The paper does not provide a numerical comparison or a rigorous proof that the constants lead to a smaller confidence interval for the same $n$.

comments.title: Missing justification for polynomial estimator mapping
comments.explanation: In Theorem 2, the authors claim this specific $f_{\phi}$ provides an unbiased estimator for any polynomial $\phi$. However, the original GPK definition (Def 2) involves a product $\phi_i K_{ij} \phi_j$. If $\phi$ depends on both $p$ and $q$, the unbiased estimator for the product requires samples independent of each other for indices $i$ and $j$. The authors briefly mention splitting the data into $n_1, n_2$, but the non-trivial leap from Bernstein polynomials of single variables to the unbiased estimation of the quadratic form $v^T K v$ is underspecified in the main text.

zero_shot__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Zero Shot (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
zero_shot__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback: The paper presents a thoughtful framework for generalized probability kernels (GPK) on discrete distributions, connecting RKHS-based methods like MMD with plugin estimation via Bernstein polynomials. The core idea of viewing MMD and its extensions as probability kernels is well-motivated and has conceptual merit. The derivation of unbiased estimators for polynomial GPKs using existing Bernstein polynomial techniques is technically sound and provides a useful bridge between distribution property estimation and kernel methods. However, there are several issues in notation, alignment between definitions and claims, and some overstatements about performance that need clarification. While the framework is promising, especially with the introduction of power-MMD, the theoretical claims—particularly around improved performance—require more careful qualification.

comments.title: Incorrect matrix transpose placement in GPK definition
comments.explanation: The expression writes _φ ◦_ ( _**p** ,_ _**q**_ ) _Kφ ◦_ ( _**q** ,_ _**p**_ ) _[T]_, suggesting the transpose applies to the entire product _φ ◦_ ( _**q** ,_ _**p**_ ). But the expanded sum shows ∑_i ∑_j [φ ◦ (p,q)]_i K_ij [φ ◦ (q,p)]_j, which corresponds to _φ ◦_ ( _**p** ,_ _**q**_ )^T K _φ ◦_ ( _**q** ,_ _**p**_ ). The transpose should apply to the first vector, not the second. This is likely a typo or misplacement of the transpose notation. In standard quadratic form, v^T K w requires v to be transposed. Given the expansion matches the correct quadratic form, this seems to be a notational error rather than a mathematical one, but it could mislead a careful reader.

comments.title: Ambiguity in degree-o polynomial mapping definition
comments.explanation: The expression uses _**[p]**[l]_ and _**[q]**[s], which appears to mean componentwise power (p_i^l, q_i^s), but this is not explicitly defined. While this is conventional in some contexts, it's potentially ambiguous because **p**[l] could be misinterpreted as indexing rather than exponentiation, especially given earlier use of bracket notation for indexing (e.g., pi). Context from later examples (e.g., Definition 6 using **p**[ρ]) suggests exponentiation, but the notation is underspecified. This ambiguity could mislead a reader unfamiliar with the authors’ conventions, particularly in a symbolic context where **p**[l] more naturally reads as indexing.

comments.title: Incorrect implication of performance improvement over existing MMD
comments.quote: we provide a tighter bound for the test statistic, thus we will certainly have a better performance using power-MMD.
comments.explanation: The authors claim a 'tighter bound' compared to Gretton et al. (2012) and conclude 'certainly have better performance'. However, the bound in Corollary 3.2 is derived under identical assumptions and sample splitting as the linear-time MMD_l. The bound they derive matches the known form up to constants, and without empirical or asymptotic power analysis, claiming 'certainly better performance' is an overstatement. While tighter constants are possible, performance in two-sample testing depends on test power under alternatives, not just deviation bounds under the null. The claim overreaches what is shown, especially since no power analysis is provided for ρ > 1.

comments.title: Inconsistency in kernel matrix symmetry assumption
comments.quote: K is a semipositive definite matrix, thus by definition of positive definite matrix, _**v** K_ _**v**[T] ≥_ 0, where equality holds if and only if _**v**_ = **0**
comments.explanation: The proof of Theorem 1 assumes K is positive definite, but earlier text only specifies that K is generated by a kernel RK(vi,vj), making it positive semidefinite. A positive semidefinite matrix does not satisfy '_**v** K _**v**[T] = 0 iff _**v**_ = 0_' — this requires strict positive definiteness. Without assuming the kernel is characteristic or the support points are such that K is full rank, this equivalence does not hold. This creates a gap in the proof: equality in the quadratic form could hold even for nonzero _φ ◦_ ( _**p** ,_ _**q**_ ) if K is singular. The authors likely intended to assume a characteristic kernel, but this is not stated.

comments.title: Underspecification of function fφ in plugin-estimator definition
comments.quote: where fφ is a function related to function φ, and K is the gram matrix brought by F.
comments.explanation: At this point, no explicit link is given between φ and fφ. While later defined in Theorem 2, Definition 7 introduces fφ without specifying how it depends on φ, leaving the plug-in estimator underspecified. A reader cannot reproduce the estimator from this definition alone. The dependency is later resolved via Theorem 2 and the Bernstein polynomial construction, but Definition 7 should minimally reference that fφ is constructed to yield unbiasedness, especially since this is a core contribution.

comments.title: Misplaced square root in test statistic for two-sample test
comments.explanation: In Corollary 3.3, the test statistic involves  GPK _E_ [ ~~_√_~~ _FZ,φρ,X,Y_ ] , where the square root symbol appears misplaced and garbled. Given the context, it likely refers to the estimator (not a square root), but the notation is corrupted. The rejection threshold is written as involving log(2/α) without any scaling by sample size, which contradicts standard MMD bounds that depend on 1/sqrt(n). This suggests a missing normalization factor; otherwise, the threshold does not vanish as sample size increases. This undermines the practical usability of the test as stated.

comments.title: Inadequate justification for numerical stability claim
comments.quote: GPK _[′] E_[will][be][much][more][numerical][stable][than] GPK _E_ [ _F, φρ, X, Y_ ]
comments.explanation: The authors define a normalized estimator GPK' [E] and claim it is 'much more numerically stable', but provide no analysis, bounds, or empirical evidence. While the motivation (exponential decay in gρ) is valid, the claim about stability of the new form is asserted without justification. The derivation skips key steps showing how CN and SN are used to stabilize the computation. This is an underspecified method: the reader cannot assess or reproduce the improved estimator’s properties.

zero_shot__glm-4.6.label: Zero Shot (glm-4.6)
zero_shot__glm-4.6.overall_feedback: The paper introduces an interesting framework of Generalized Probability Kernel (GPK) that unifies various discrepancy measures for discrete distributions. The connection to kernel methods and the proposal of power-MMD as a natural extension of MMD are conceptually appealing. However, the paper contains several mathematical and notational issues that undermine its technical rigor. The most serious problem is in the core definition of GPK, which appears mathematically inconsistent. Additionally, there are gaps in justification for key theoretical claims and ambiguities in notation that could confuse readers. While the overall direction is promising, these issues need to be addressed before the technical contributions can be properly evaluated.

comments.title: Mathematical inconsistency in GPK definition
comments.explanation: The definition appears mathematically inconsistent. If φ ◦ (p,q) is a vector in R^k, then φ ◦ (p,q) K φ ◦ (q,p)ᵀ should be a scalar computed as v^T K w where v = φ ◦ (p,q) and w = φ ◦ (q,p). However, the double sum expression shown doesn't match this - it uses φ ◦ (p_i,q_i) and φ ◦ (q_j,p_j) which suggests element-wise application of φ, but then the indices don't align correctly with matrix multiplication. The notation is confusing and the mathematical expression seems incorrect. This is a fundamental issue in the core definition of the proposed kernel.

comments.title: Incorrect property of semi-positive definite matrix
comments.quote: _K_ is a semipositive definite matrix, thus by definition of positive definite matrix, _**v** K_ _**v**[T] ≥_ 0, where equality holds if and only if _**v**_ = **0**
comments.explanation: This statement is incorrect. For a semi-positive definite matrix, v^T K v = 0 does NOT necessarily imply v = 0. The matrix can have non-trivial nullspace. The correct statement would be that v^T K v ≥ 0 for all v, and equality holds if and only if v is in the nullspace of K. Since K is constructed from a kernel function on the support points, it could indeed have a non-trivial nullspace unless the kernel is strictly positive definite. This error undermines the proof of Theorem 1.

comments.title: Ambiguous notation in polynomial GPK definition
comments.explanation: The notation here is unclear. What does **p**[l] mean? If it's element-wise power, it should be written as (p_i)^l. The double sum notation with [] and undefined indices makes it hard to parse the intended mathematical expression. The authors need to clarify whether this is meant to be a vector where each component is a polynomial in p_i and q_i, or something else entirely.

comments.title: Undefined notation in power-MMD
comments.explanation: The notation **p**[ρ] is not defined anywhere in the paper. Based on context, it likely means element-wise power (p_i^ρ), but this should be explicitly stated. Without clear definition, readers might misinterpret this as some other operation on the vector p.

comments.title: Underspecified plugin-estimator
comments.quote: _We define the plugin-estimator of_ GPK _F ,φ_ ( _**p** ,_ _**q**_ ) _as_ GPK _E_ [ _F, φ, X, Y_ ] = _fφ_ ( _S_ _i,n_ 1 _, S_ _i,m_ 1) _K fφ_ ( _S_ _i,n_ 2 _, S_ _i,m_ 2) _[T]_
comments.explanation: The function fφ is referenced but never properly defined in the main text. While Definition 8 refers to it as 'the default-plugin-estimator', the actual form of fφ is only given in Theorem 2 without proper derivation. The transition from the kernel definition to the estimator form lacks sufficient explanation for readers to understand how the plugin estimator is constructed.

comments.title: Questionable deviation bound
comments.explanation: The bound appears overly optimistic. The denominator Z^2 involves terms like τ^2 which themselves depend on the kernel matrix K and sample sizes. The bound doesn't seem to properly account for the variance of the estimator, especially for cases where the kernel matrix has large eigenvalues. Without seeing the full proof (referenced to appendix), this bound seems suspiciously tight compared to known concentration inequalities for kernel-based statistics.

comments.title: Incorrect hypothesis test threshold
comments.quote: A hypothesis test of level α for the null hypothesis _**p**_ = _**q** has the acceptance region  GPK _E_ [ ~~_√_~~ _FZ,φρ,X,Y_ ]  _<_  12 _[log]_[(]  _α_ 2  _−_ 1)
comments.explanation: The threshold √(1/2 log(2/α - 1)) doesn't match standard hypothesis testing formulations. Typically, for a level-α test based on a concentration bound, we'd expect something like √(log(2/α)/(2Z^2)) or similar. The given expression appears incorrect and doesn't follow from the concentration inequality stated in Theorem 3.

comments.title: Insufficient justification for numerical stability claims
comments.quote: For the question of numerical stability, since _gρ_ ( _Ni, n_ ) _≤_  _Nni_  _ρ_ , the term will exponentially decrease with the increase of _ρ_ . This effect will cause numerical problem when _Ni ≪ n_ and _ρ_ is large.
comments.explanation: The authors claim numerical issues for ρ > 1 but don't provide sufficient analysis or empirical evidence. The proposed alternative GPK'_E is introduced without theoretical justification for why it would be more stable or how it relates to the original statistic. More rigorous analysis of the numerical properties and potential solutions is needed.

zero_shot__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Zero Shot (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
zero_shot__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback: The paper introduces a generalized probability kernel (GPK) framework for discrete distributions and uses Bernstein polynomial techniques to construct unbiased estimators, specifically applying this to a proposed 'power-MMD'. While the mathematical motivation is compelling and the connection between Bernstein polynomials and kernel-based statistics is a valuable contribution, the paper suffers from inconsistent notation, several missing definitions for key variables (e.g., in the convergence bounds), and some OCR/transcription errors in the provided LaTeX text. The clarity of the theoretical results is hampered by these presentation issues, making the paper difficult to reproduce despite an interesting core idea.

comments.title: Inconsistent definition of GPK summation range
comments.quote: GPK_{F,\phi}(\mathbf{p},\mathbf{q}) = \phi \circ (\mathbf{p}, \mathbf{q}) K \phi \circ (\mathbf{q}, \mathbf{p}) [T] = [] i\in [k]  j\in [k] [\phi][ ◦][(] [p][i][, q][i][)] [K][ij][\phi][ ◦][(] [q][j][, p][j][)]
comments.explanation: The notation becomes highly corrupted in the final expansion. While the intent is a quadratic form $\sum_{i,j=1}^k K_{ij} \phi(p_i, q_i) \phi(q_j, p_j)$, the expression is physically broken by brackets and nonsensical symbols. A reader must infer the intent from the matrix multiplication form, but the definition is technically underspecified by these artifacts.

comments.title: Notation mismatch for polynomial degree
comments.quote: where o \in Z is the degree of the polynomial, and al,s \in R is the coefficient
comments.explanation: The symbol 'o' is used as the variable for the degree, but in the summation definition $\sum_{l=0}^o \dots$, it appears to be a variable name that conflicts with common mathematical notation conventions and is likely a transcription error (possibly intended to be $\rho$ or $d$). Given the later usage of $\rho$ in power-MMD, this creates ambiguity.

comments.title: Underspecified variables in convergence bound
comments.quote: Pr( | GPK_E [ F, \phi, X, Y ] - E[GPK_E [ F, \phi, X, Y ]] | ≥ a ) ≤ 2 e[-][2] Z[a][2]
comments.explanation: The variable 'Z' is used in the exponent of the concentration inequality, but it is not defined in Section 4.2. In Theorem 3, Z appears to be a function, but it is never explicitly formulated before the inequality is presented. This is a significant omission for a theoretical result.

comments.title: Definition of g_ρ is missing
comments.quote: any member GPK_{F, φρ} (√ p, q) in power-MMD has a default-plugin-estimator with the mapping function f_φ(Si,n1, Si,m1) = g_ρ(Ni, n1) - g_ρ(Mi, m1)
comments.explanation: The function $g_{ρ}$ is referenced repeatedly throughout Section 5 to define the estimator, but it is never formally defined in the main text. While a reader can infer it relates to the Bernstein estimation of powers, the lack of an explicit definition makes reproducing the estimator impossible.

zero_shot__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Zero Shot (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
zero_shot__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: The paper proposes a generalized probability kernel (GPK) framework and focuses on polynomial GPKs, particularly power-MMD, for two-sample testing. The theoretical motivation is interesting and connects discrete distribution estimation with kernel methods. However, the manuscript suffers from significant technical gaps: core formulas for the plugin estimator, deviation bounds, and hypothesis test thresholds are missing or severely corrupted, making the methodology unreproducible. Additionally, the proof of Theorem 1 contains a fundamental linear algebra error conflating PSD and PD matrices, and the authors make unjustified claims that tighter concentration bounds directly imply better hypothesis test performance. While the high-level idea is sound, these issues must be rigorously addressed before the work can be evaluated for publication.

comments.title: Conflation of PSD and PD matrices in Theorem 1 proof
comments.quote: K is a semipositive definite matrix, thus by definition of positive definite matrix, _**v** K_ _**v**[T] ≥_ 0, where equality holds if and only if _**v**_ = **0** , and since _**v**_ = _φ ◦_ ( _**p** ,_ _**q**_ ), this condition further means _φ ◦_ ( _**p** ,_ _**q**_ ) = 0, which holds if and only if _**p**_ = _**q**_ .
comments.explanation: I initially skimmed this proof and assumed the authors implicitly assumed K is positive definite, which is true for universal kernels on distinct points. However, the text explicitly states 'semipositive definite' and then immediately claims 'by definition of positive definite matrix' that the quadratic form is zero iff v=0. This is a mathematical contradiction: PSD matrices can have zero quadratic forms for non-zero v in their null space. The proof must either explicitly state that K is positive definite (which requires noting that the support points are distinct) or the statement that equality holds iff v=0 is false. This undermines the formal justification for the discrepancy property.

comments.title: Missing/Broken plugin estimator and deviation bound formulas
comments.quote:
We define the plugin-estimator of_ GPK _F ,φ_ ( _**p** ,_ _**q**_ ) _as_ 


_where fφ is a function related to function φ, and K is the gram matrix brought by F._
comments.explanation: The explicit formula for the plugin estimator is completely absent. While I initially suspected OCR corruption, the subsequent claims in Theorem 2 and Corollary 3.1 also reference missing equations for f_φ, Z, and the convergence bound. The unbiased estimator for a polynomial mapping should be constructed via linearity using unbiased estimators for monomials (e.g., g_ρ(N,n) from Qian et al.). Without the exact expression for f_φ and the variance constant Z in the McDiarmid bound, a reader cannot verify the unbiased claim, reproduce the estimator, or compute the required thresholds. This is a critical gap for reproducibility.

comments.title: Ill-defined hypothesis test threshold in Corollary 3.3
comments.quote: GPK _E_ [ ~~_√_~~ _FZ,φρ,X,Y_ ]  _<_  12 _[log]_[(]  _α_ 2  _−_ 1) Where Z is defined in Corollary 3.1
comments.explanation: The acceptance region threshold for the two-sample test is presented in a garbled form that lacks the necessary bound constant from McDiarmid's inequality. McDiarmid's inequality yields a threshold of the form √(C/2 log(2/α)), where C = Σ c_i². The corollary references 'Z' but does not define it in the visible text, and the formula structure itself is broken. Even if we assume OCR artifacts for symbols like ~_√_~, the structural dependence on the Lipschitz/vbound constant is missing. Without it, the test statistic's critical value is mathematically incomplete, making the hypothesis test impossible to implement as stated.

comments.title: Unjustified claim that tighter bounds imply better test performance
comments.quote: According to Remark 3.2, we provide a tighter bound for the test statistic, thus we will certainly have a better performance using power-MMD.
comments.explanation: I read this statement and was immediately concerned. A tighter concentration inequality only provides a more conservative (or precise) confidence interval for the estimator under the null. It does not automatically translate to 'better performance' in hypothesis testing, which depends on statistical power, type II error, and the separation between distributions under the alternative. For ρ=1, the power-MMD statistic is mathematically identical to the linear-time MMD^2_l statistic. Claiming 'certainly better performance' based solely on a deviation bound is an overstatement that lacks theoretical or empirical justification.

comments.title: Non-standard and inconsistent notation for polynomial degree
comments.quote: where o ∈_Z _is the degree of the polynomial, and al,s ∈_ R _is the coefficient
comments.explanation: The symbol `o` is used to denote the degree of the polynomial. In standard mathematical literature, polynomial degree is typically denoted by d, ρ, or O. Moreover, `o` later appears in 'o φ (p, q)' in Section 4.1, where it clearly functions as an OCR artifact for φ_∘. This dual usage creates ambiguity and violates consistency. I recommend replacing `o` with a standard Greek or Latin letter (e.g., ρ or d) and ensuring it is consistently defined throughout.

comments.title: Problematic numerical stability solution via deterministic upper bounds
comments.quote: For the question of numerical stability, since _gρ_ ( _Ni, n_ ) _≤_  _Nni_  _ρ_ , the term will exponentially decrease with the increase of _ρ_ . This effect will cause numerical problem when _Ni ≪ n_ and _ρ_ is large. One solution is to find an upper-bound of  GPK _E_ [ ~~_√_~~ _FZ,φρ,X,Y_ ]  which is numerical stable.
comments.explanation: The authors correctly identify underflow when raising small empirical frequencies to high powers. However, they propose replacing the test statistic with a deterministic upper bound GPK_E' (Corollary 3.4) to ensure stability. Replacing an unbiased, data-driven statistic with a fixed or distribution-dependent upper bound fundamentally alters its statistical properties: it introduces severe bias, invalidates the concentration bounds derived earlier, and changes the null distribution. The paper does not explain how to calibrate the threshold for this modified statistic or how it affects type I error control. This solution is theoretically unsound for hypothesis testing and requires substantial justification or a different stability approach (e.g., log-transforms or normalization).

zero_shot__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Zero Shot (deepseek-v4-flash)
zero_shot__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback: The paper introduces a generalized probability kernel (GPK) framework for discrete distributions and proposes power-MMD for two-sample testing. However, the paper suffers from several critical issues: the main theorem (Theorem 2) stating the existence of unbiased estimators is incomplete in the main text, making the key contribution unclear; the hypothesis test acceptance region in Corollary 3.3 is garbled and likely in error; the paper claims experimental results but none are presented; and several derivations lack sufficient detail or contain questionable claims. These issues significantly undermine the paper's contribution and reproducibility.

comments.title: Incomplete statement of Theorem 2
comments.explanation: The theorem statement cuts off after 'mapping function fφ to be:' with no formula shown. This is a major omission as the theorem is central to the paper's contribution on unbiased estimation for polynomial GPK. The reader cannot discern what the estimator is. The appendix provides a sketch but relies on the missing definition. This must be corrected.

comments.title: Garbled and likely erroneous hypothesis test acceptance region
comments.explanation: Corollary 3.3 gives an acceptance region for the test. The OCR presents it as '| GPK_E / √Z | < √(1/2 log(2/α) - 1)', which is nonsensical: the '-1' inside the square root and the division by √Z are inconsistent with standard concentration bounds. The correct form should involve √(Z/2 log(2/α)). This error would mislead readers attempting to implement the test. Additionally, the notation '√Z' is ambiguous because Z as defined is a sum of squared finite-difference bounds, not a variance. The derivation should be clarified.

comments.title: Missing experimental results
comments.explanation: The paper mentions 'experiment section' and claims to focus on datasets with known relativity measures, but the provided text contains no experimental section. Without experiments, claims about practical performance of power-MMD are unsupported. The paper appears incomplete.

comments.title: Unverified equivalence claim to linear time MMD
comments.explanation: Remark 3.1 states that the default-plugin-estimator for ρ=1 is equivalent to the linear time MMD statistic from Gretton et al. (2012). The paper's estimator uses empirical frequencies from four separate samples, whereas the linear time MMD uses paired observations. The equivalence is not obvious and is not derived in the text. This claim requires justification; otherwise, it may be incorrect.

comments.title: Insufficient derivation of deviation bounds
comments.explanation: The derivation of the deviation bound in Corollary 3.2 is unclear and appears to contain algebraic missteps. The bound expression for τ_n is given as 'n^{ρ-1} sup_i ∑_j K_{ij}' but the preceding inequality seems to involve summing over i,j. The justification for the final simplified bound is not provided, making it hard to verify correctness. This weakens the theoretical support for the test.

zero_shot__glm-4.7-flash.label: Zero Shot (glm-4.7-flash)
zero_shot__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback: The paper proposes a Generalized Probability Kernel (GPK) framework and a specific 'power-MMD' statistic designed for two-sample testing. While the unifying perspective is conceptually interesting, the manuscript contains a critical definition error: the proposed kernel formula in Definition 2 yields the negative of the statistics it claims to represent (e.g., MMD), creating a sign inconsistency that must be resolved. Additionally, the conclusion overstates the performance of power-MMD, directly contradicting the text's admission that the impact of the power parameter ρ remains unclear. These issues are fundamental and prevent the paper from being accepted without significant revision.
